issue_title: Conda should use the Accept-Encoding: gzip header rather than loading the repodata.json.bz2 url directly
issue_body:
You can see here that by using the `Accept-Encoding: gzip` we can let nginx handle the compression: 

```
curl -I -H "Accept-Encoding: gzip" https://conda.anaconda.org/sean/osx-64/repodata.json

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Encoding: gzip
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Tue, 21 Jun 2016 15:44:15 GMT
license_expired: False
license_valid: True
Server: nginx/1.8.1
Set-Cookie: session=eyJfaWQiOnsiIGIiOiJOR1ZoTURBd05tTTBaRE16TURjME0yWmpPVGxrWVRWalpHTXpNamN6TW1ZPSJ9fQ.CkryTw.sDb8oRPUTzpbK6SEaykdSnY__e8; Domain=.anaconda.org; HttpOnly; Path=/
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000
Connection: keep-alive
```

After running a simple comparison of the two endpoints, It looks like this will have a marginal improvement on performance.

# BZ2 in process

```
time curl https://conda.anaconda.org/anaconda/osx-64/repodata.json.bz2 | dd > /dev/null 
  % Total    % Received % Xferd  Average Speed   Time    Time     Time  Current
                                 Dload  Upload   Total   Spent    Left  Speed
100  401k  100  401k    0     0  62482      0  0:00:06  0:00:06 --:--:--  110k

803+1 records out

real	0m6.591s
user	0m0.029s
sys	0m0.022s
```

# Nginx gzip 
```
time curl  -H "Accept-Encoding: gzip" https://conda.anaconda.org/anaconda/osx-64/repodata.json | dd > /dev/null 
  % Total    % Received % Xferd  Average Speed   Time    Time     Time  Current
```

```
$ conda env create --file testml.yaml 
Using Anaconda API: https://api.anaconda.org

SpecNotFound: Can't process without a name
```

Someone on the ML pointed out the issue: spelling mistake in the YAML.  I'd suggest a few things:

* improve the error message to say something like:

    ```
    "dependencies" section not found, unable to create conda environment
    ```

* report any sections that are found that are unexpected and are going to be ignored (such as `dependecies`

* use something like Kwalify (and perhaps [pykwalify](https://github.com/Grokzen/pykwalify) ) to test/assert an open schema on the JSON for env files

issue_title: libxml2 library issue on osx
issue_body:
I am trying to run [nikola](https://getnikola.com/) on osx. Rather than creating a virtualenv as recommended, I am trying to create a conda environment. I have hit some dependency issues that have me stuck.

You can see my environment.yml here:
https://gist.github.com/rabernat/37db7f98c9903fe9664a2a390a06c9e1
(Note I have tried with and without the conda-forge channel.)

Within this environment, when I try to run nikola, I get the following error:
```bash
$ nikola
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/rpa/anaconda/envs/nikola/bin/nikola", line 7, in <module>
    from nikola.__main__ import main
  File "/Users/rpa/anaconda/envs/nikola/lib/python3.5/site-packages/nikola/__init__.py", line 35, in <module>
    from .nikola import Nikola  # NOQA
  File "/Users/rpa/anaconda/envs/nikola/lib/python3.5/site-packages/nikola/nikola.py", line 54, in <module>
    import lxml.etree
ImportError: dlopen(/Users/rpa/anaconda/envs/nikola/lib/python3.5/site-packages/lxml/etree.cpython-35m-darwin.so, 2): Library not loaded: libxml2.2.dylib
  Referenced from: /Users/rpa/anaconda/envs/nikola/lib/python3.5/site-packages/lxml/etree.cpython-35m-darwin.so
  Reason: Incompatible library version: etree.cpython-35m-darwin.so requires version 12.0.0 or later, but libxml2.2.dylib provides version 10.0.0
```

It seems to be a libxml2 issue, as described here:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/23172384/lxml-runtime-error-reason-incompatible-library-version-etree-so-requires-vers

Curiously, I can overcome this issue by setting
```bash
DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH=/Users/rpa/anaconda/envs/nikola/lib
```

However, it is my understanding that this should not be necessary.

Any recommendations for how to move forward?

issue_title: [Discussion] Would it be valuable to allow configuring a default resource group?
issue_body:
I wonder whether it wouldn't be valuable to allow users to configure a default resource group, either per-user, per-shell or per-directory (via some kind of config file), and would take effect for all commands where a resource group wasn't explicitly specified. I've seen this kind of behavior provided by other CLIs, with the goal of reducing the number of repetitive parameters, especially if they don't change frequently or are context-sensitive (e.g. can be derived via a config file in the CWD).

I'm not entirely sure how devs use resource groups in the wild, but it seems like it could be a useful simplification of their workflow by being able to configure a resource group for a session, project, etc. and then omit it from there on out. That way the CLI can strike a balance between flexibility and verbosity.

issue_title: [WebApps] [Discussion] Could the runtime list be more "real-time"?
issue_body:
### Description

Node 8 support was just deployed to Web Apps (on Windows), and I noticed that calling `az webapp list-runtimes` doesn't reflect that (`node|7.7` is the latest Node version displayed). Additionally, Node 6.10 has been available in Linux Web Apps for a while, but running `az webapp list-runtimes --linux` displays `node|6.9` as the latest.

It would be ideal if users could rely solely on this command to understand the set of available runtimes, as opposed to needing to keep up with blogs/Twitter/etc. in order to find out that something is available.

Additionally, in the case of Linux runtimes, it's kind of strange that it states that the list is an "offline" copy, and that you can visit `https://aka.ms/linux-stacks` for the full list. Not only does that seem fairly inconvenient, but the list of available runtimes isn't even up-to-date on that page either! So the user would navigate to the page, only to get the exact same list. I sent a [PR](https://github.com/Microsoft/azure-docs/pull/1898) to update that page 3 days ago, and haven't seen much traction, so while we could try to keep the docs up to date, it just feels like having a really realiable `az webapp list-runtimes` command would be a lot nicer.

issue_title: SDK 2.0 QueueService class instances not pickleable - QueueService can't be used in Python MP environment
issue_body:
Instances of the QueueService class in the 2.0 SDK appear to not be serializable by the pickle module in Python 2.7, and therefore it can't be used in a Python MP context (eg. in Process objects). The default decoding/encoding callback functions appear to be implemented in a static class QueueMessageFormat using @staticmethod methods, which appear not to be pickleable. 
A workaround is to implement custom "non-static" callbacks and set the instance fields in the QueueService object to these custom callbacks.

Perhaps this design pattern should be changed so the QueueService class is pickleable by default, or guidance could be given to implement your own encode/decode callbacks (forgive me if this is already documented).

Platform: Windows
Python version: 2.7.9 +

Reproducible source code scenario distilled from SDK code:

```python
import pickle

class QueueMessageFormat:
    @staticmethod
    def noencode(data):
        return data     
    @staticmethod
    def nodecode(data):
        return data
class QueueService(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.encode_function = QueueMessageFormat.noencode
        self.decode_function = QueueMessageFormat.nodecode
def main():
    f=QueueService()
    with open("pickleme.txt","wb") as output:
        pickle.dump(f,output)
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```
This example yields "pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle <function noencode at 0x02880930>: it's not found as __main__.noencode"

blob

### What problem was encountered?
when using set_container_acl() to set a stored access policy to container, it always clear all the existing container access policies first, then set the new policy.

here is my code:
def generate_SAS():

        service = BlockBlobService(account_name=account_name, account_key=account_key)

        # Set access policy on container

        identifiers = {id_number: access_policy}
        
        acl = service.set_container_acl(container_name, signed_identifiers=identifiers)
       
        # Wait 30 seconds for acl to propagate
        time.sleep(30)
       
        # Indicates to use the access policy set on the container

        print('READ TOKEN IS :')

# Main method.

    account_key    =os.environ['STORAGE_ACCOUNT_KEY']
    container_name =os.environ['STORAGE_CONTAINER_NAME']
    days_until_expiry = 365*5

no

issue_title: Add interface to set connection and read timeout
issue_body:
Some environment are more exposed to network congestion or JVM stalls, for example from full GC. However it costs more when the client abandons the query and submits it again, than to have longer timeouts at the socket level. Batch computations are usually less latency sensitive and more tolerant to higher timeouts.

`requests`allows to set the sockets connection and read timeouts. The `dbapi.Cursor` object should provide an interface to set them.

issue_title: Pytest: Allow coroutine fixtures
issue_body:
[pytest-asyncio](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-asyncio) provides a very nice feature, which is to allow coroutine fixtures. For example it makes possible to create a fixture making asynchronous DB requests.

Unfortunately pytest-asyncio and pytest-aiohttp seems to no longer be compatible. So far this is the only feature that I'm missing, so it would be awesome if it were added to pytest-aiohttp.

Here is the implementation in pytest-asyncio:
https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-asyncio/blob/92b34e5e9a14c45062d6ce2e2686ed8b11c7c03e/pytest_asyncio/plugin.py#L52-L134

I've tried to adapt it to pytest-aiohttp but unsuccessfully so far.

issue_title: aiohttp webserver with python 3.7 broken
issue_body:
I have python3.7-dev and python-nightly in my CI script. Recently they started failing when I call  app.router.add_get()

This is a minimal reproducer, works on 3.6 and fails on 3.7-dev
```
import re

r = re.compile(re.escape('/foo'))
print(r)
print(r.pattern)
assert r.pattern.startswith('\\/')
```

issue_title: Cursor.execute returns a pyodbc.Cursor instead of itself
issue_body:
Python 3.6
aioodbc 0.1.0
Windows
SQLite

The following just can't work
```python
async def example():
    async aioodbc.connect(...) as conn, conn.cursor() as cursor:
        async for row in await cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM some_table"):
            print(row)
```

Currently for it to work I have to use two lines
```python
async def example():
    async aioodbc.connect(...) as conn, conn.cursor() as cursor:
        cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM some_table")
        async for row in await cursor:
            print(row)
```
or not use async for loop but that defeats the purpose of async.

issue_title: How can I make the assistant run on start up on a raspberry pi?
Better message for retrying
issue_body:
The current error message printed when [retrying the grpc connection](https://github.com/googlesamples/assistant-sdk-python/commit/7da41cfbcd4f5aa0e0e34aa01a1ea1fdfb04b83b) make it looks like something bad happened.

```
INFO:root:Recording audio request.
ERROR:root:grpc unavailable error: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with (StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE, Connect Failed)>
INFO:root:Recording audio request.
```

The level should be switched to warning and more explicit about the fact that the gRPC call is retried

issue_title: Replace webpack with require.js
issue_body:
Proposal from #1547: go back to using require.js for this notebook, and leave webpack for newer projects, such as jupyterlab.

The switch to webpack in the legacy notebook seems to make lots of backward-compatibility maintenance impossible, and has been the source of numerous issues (#1547, #1439, #1431, etc.). Following some discussion in #1547, I think it's not feasible to maintain our goals of backward-compatibility in the existing notebook javascript while adopting webpack. The key point being that it seems to be impossible to include a module in the webpack, and then load it later with require, which is a necessity for numerous extensions and official APIs.

If that's not true, and we are just failing to configure things correctly, there might be a nicer, simpler fix. But this gist is, for the notebook js the following two things must return the same object:
- `require('notebook/js/cell')` in our packed-up sources
- `require('notebook/js/cell')` at runtime, in outputs, custom.js, or nbextensions

(and so on, for codemirror and everything else we load via require). The behavior in master is to load a different copy of the module, which is causing all kinds of things to break.

issue_title: Internal server error on notebook exporting
issue_body:
Hi,

When trying to export [this notebook](http://verney.lv/phynum/homework1.ipynb) to PDF (with LaTeX), I get the following error (500 internal server error):

```
When looking at the server logs, I found this:
! Undefined control sequence.
    <argument> \ImportTok
                          {import} \NormalTok {math, numpy}
    l.550 \ImportTok{import} \NormalTok{math, numpy}

    ?
    ! Emergency stop.
    <argument> \ImportTok
                          {import} \NormalTok {math, numpy}
    l.550 \ImportTok{import} \NormalTok{math, numpy}

    !  ==> Fatal error occurred, no output PDF file produced!
    Transcript written on notebook.log.
```

I should have all the necessary dependencies and tried it on two different systems, which can export normally to PDF other notebooks.

Thanks

issue_title: 404 on MathJax/config/Safe.js
issue_body:
I'm getting a 404 starting up Jupyter with just a 'jupter notebook' command in Anaconda on Windows 10 (though the same thing happens to my colleague on Windows 8).

```
404 GET /static/components/MathJax/config/Safe.js?rev=2.5.3 (::1) 7.03ms referer=http://localhost:8889/notebooks/Testing/Untitled5.ipynb
```
Nothing else loads in the notebook after that, so it's unusable.

I also get the same issues when starting it with 'ipython notebook'

issue_title: Feature request: Make Sorting by last modified configurable as default
issue_body:
I prefer to have my notebooks sorted by "last modified" timestamp, so I'd like to be able to have this sorting as default, after launching a new notebook server.
Having this available could possibly solve #1218 as well, maybe?

issue_title: Add "Must bump version" check
issue_body:
This is a suggestion for a new check:

When doing a PR, it should bump the module's version should be bumped, either if we are porting, adding feature, or fixing code.
The check should run only for PRs.

Special case: some PR might not involve modules (e.g: changir .travis.yml) - in these case the check should not fail.

* Header.events -> Header.stream

issue_title: Kafka, getting that first message.
issue_body:
When trying to write an integration test for Kafka, I noticed I was frequently missing that first message.  I think I've finally gotten the hang of it by changing my subscribe call to something blocking like this:

```
def assignment(consumer, partitions):
   print('Assignment:', partitions)
   assignment.isAssigned=True


def test_consume():
   ...
   assignment.isAssigned=False
   c.subscribe( [ topic ], assignment )    
   while not assignment.isAssigned:
      print "Waiting for Subscription"
      msg=c.poll( timeout=1.0 )
```

Does this make sense? Or can someone elaborate? It seems like the subscribe call is effectively Asynchronous, and that if I don't wait for it, my 'offset' will be wrong.

issue_title: Fetching full user list from large supergroup always yields 10k users
issue_body:
I'm trying to fetch the full user list from a supergroup that has well over 10k users, but I always get 10k users back. Here's an example to illustrate that:
```
>>> def get_channel_participants(channel, offset, batch_size):
...     api_id = 1234567
...     api_hash = '7dfs7sdf7sdf7sdf7sdfsdf7sd7f'
...     client = TelegramClient('session_name', api_id, api_hash)
...     client.start()
...     all_participants = []
...
...     while True:
...         participants = client(GetParticipantsRequest(
...             channel, ChannelParticipantsSearch(''), offset, batch_size,
...             hash=0
...         ))
...         if not participants.users:
...             break
...         all_participants.extend(participants.users)
...         offset += len(participants.users)
...         sleep(5)
...     return all_participants
...
>>> users = get_channel_participants('mysupergroup', 0, 100)                                   
>>> len(users)
10000
>>> users = get_channel_participants('mysupergroup', 0, 500)                                   
>>> len(users)
10000
>>> users = get_channel_participants('mysupergroup', 0, 20000)                                   
>>> len(users)
10000
```
Is there a hard cap somewhere in the telethon code/telegram api that I've missed? If so, is there a way to somehow get the **full** user list and not just 10000 users?

issue_title: No contributing / developer docs
issue_body:
The README appears to be end user focused and the Wiki is empty, so there doesn't appear to be any documentation on how to contribute to the project or to hack on the code.

I can figure out some of the dependencies by looking at the Makefile (e.g. pep8, pylint, 
mypy), but explicit documentation on what developers need to install, what tests should be run before submitting PRs, etc would be useful.

Also, background documentation for things which might not be readily apparent to your garden variety Pythonista such was why it's a good thing to pretend to do static typing in a dynamically typed language, how to install `mypy` (since they don't appear to believe in installation instructions), etc

```
cwlVersion: v1.0
class: Workflow
inputs:
  input_file:
    type: File?
outputs: []
steps:

    run: cat.cwl
    in:
      input_file: input_file
    out: []
```

issue_title: Attempt to fetch failed: :closed
issue_body:
We are receiving this vague error for certain URLs when using Watson's NLU API. I saw in [a previously closed issue](https://github.com/watson-developer-cloud/python-sdk/issues/182) that the cause might be the site's robots.txt preventing Watson from scraping the URL. 

However, in our case, it doesn't seem to be the issue. Also, in cases when robots.txt was blocking Watson we've seen this, appropriate error message: "Request to fetch blocked: disallowed."  Could someone explain what this error means: **Attempt to fetch failed: :closed**?

Here is an example URL that yields this error: https://www.unionathletics.com/news/2017/5/1/womens-outdoor-track-field-bechu-collects-third-straight-liberty-league-performer-of-the-week-honors.aspx

And their robots.txt: https://www.unionathletics.com/robots.txt

Any help would be appreciated.

Thanks in advance,
Jason

issue_title: Powerline's bash prompt hangs upon 'cd' to certain directories
issue_body:
The Powerline bash prompt hangs upon issuing a `cd` command to a number of directories. Neither sending `SIGINT` nor `SIGTSTP` are sufficient to recover control of affected prompts. Those directories can be listed fine with the Powerline prompt and `cd` works when not using the Powerline bash prompt.

The directories that this occurs in appear to all have (valid) symbolic links contained within them. However, I cannot reproduce this issue by creating another directory with symbolic links elsewhere, so this may simply be coincidental. All directories that were confirmed to be affected by this resided within a single user-created directory, but again this is likely to be coincidental and due to a lack of systematic directory traversal.

issue_title: Nested schemas requires all validations to pass
issue_body:
If the validation of a single field in a nested schema fails, then values of the entire nested schema are thrown away. Example:

~~~
import marshmallow as ma

class Child(ma. Schema):
    a = ma.fields.Integer()
    b = ma.fields.Integer()

class Parent(ma.Schema):
    x = ma.fields.Integer()
    y = ma.fields.Integer()
    z = ma.fields.Nested(Child)

p = Parent()
~~~

If I call p.load() with a dict that has 'x' set to a valid integer, and 'y' set to None, the returned data has one value, and the error list contains on error message for 'y'

But if I do the same thing with the child instance ('z') and set for example 'a' to None, and 'b' to a valid value, then the entire nested schema ('z') is absent from the data returned by p.load()

I'm trying to use marshmallow for saving settings to disk. I do *not* want the loading method to skip an entire nested object just because one of the values of it are invalid.

Any ideas?

issue_title: Smart way of getting all SQLAlchemy Fields
issue_body:
Is there some trick to set the `fields` in the `Meta` class to all of the columns in a [SQLAlchemy model](http://marshmallow.readthedocs.org/en/latest/examples.html#quotes-api-flask-sqlalchemy)?

Right now I am using [get_columns](http://sqlalchemy-utils.readthedocs.org/en/latest/orm_helpers.html#get-columns) like this:
```
    class Meta:
        additional = get_columns(Model).keys()
```
but this doesn't seem ideal or the very least would be cool to be included in marshmallow.

issue_title: fields.Function returning _missing instead None?
issue_body:
`obj.some_method()` returns `None`. But the parameters had not been serialized with `Schema().dump()`. I tried many parameters combinations:

```
attr = fields.Function(lambda obj: obj.some_method(), default=None)
attr = fields.Function(lambda obj: obj.some_method(), missing=None)
attr = fields.Function(lambda obj: obj.some_method(), allow_none=None)
attr = fields.Function(lambda obj: obj.some_method(), default=None, missing=None)
attr = fields.Function(lambda obj: obj.some_method(), default=None, missing=None, allow_none=True)
```

I took a look into the source code, maybe there is some issue with this exception catch part:

```
    def _serialize(self, value, attr, obj):
        try:
            return self._call_or_raise(self.serialize_func, obj, attr)
        except AttributeError:  # the object is not expected to have the attribute
            pass
        return missing_
```

issue_title: Providing support for loom files in R/Bioconductor
issue_body: Some of us in Bioconductor are exploring the compatibility of `*.loom` files with our existing HDF5 data architecture. It seems feasible (potentially trivially so) based on examination of the specifications, but is it possible to obtain some actual `*.loom` files to play around with? I can't figure out where  `cortex.loom` comes from in the example code, and https://loom.linnarssonlab.org/ doesn't seem to be functional.

issue_title: Enabling unsat-cores yields wrong satisfiablity result on simple QF_UFLIRA formula
issue_body:
Hi,
I bumped in a very strange behavior with z3 4.5.0 with unsat cores.

When I check the following simple formula:
```
(declare-fun p () Int)
(declare-fun q () Int)
(declare-fun r () Real)
(declare-fun ih (Real Int) Int)
(assert (! (not (=> (and (= (to_real (- p 3)) r) (= (to_real q) r)) (or (< p (ih (to_real (- p 3)) (+ q 3))) (<= (ih r p) p)))) :named a2))
(check-sat)
(exit)
```
I correctly get an `unsat` answer from z3. But just by adding the option `(set-option :produce-unsat-cores true)` at the beginning of the file, I get a `sat` answer from the `check-sat` command!

The very same issue arises when using the python API instead of the command line.

issue_title: Should tests respect the .gangarc?
issue_body:
Hi all,

Just a quick question, should we let the test configuration as used by py.test make use of the `.gangarc` configuration file?

I only ask this as I am surprised to see settings in my .gangarc file _not_ overloaded by the test configuration.
(In particular because I've been experimenting with my gangadir in different locations I ended up with multiple tests running on the same repo which is less than ideal).

Should we bump the setting of options within the tests to be the same as a a user-set option rather than what it is now so that the test configuration always takes priority?
If the test knows it needs a certain configuration parameter having a different one from .gangarc would just lead to false errors I think an could lead to confusion.

I'm keen to hear people's thoughts on this,

Rob

issue_title: HttpParserUpgrade raised after event hooks are called.
issue_body:
The `HttpParserUpgrade` exception appears to get called after the event hooks have been called into (at least with small requests that fit into a single data_received call)

This makes it not terribly useful, as we may have already called into a handler function / issued a response / etc... by the time the upgrade is raised.

Sketch of protocol class:

    def __init__(self, ...):
        self.request_parser = httptools.HttpRequestParser(self)
        ...

        # The asyncio.Protocol hooks...
        ...

        def data_received(self, data):
            print('data_received()')
            try:
                self.request_parser.feed_data(data)
            except httptools.HttpParserUpgrade:
                print('upgrade exception')

        # Event hooks called back into by HttpRequestParser...
        ...

        def on_headers_complete(self):
            print('on_headers_complete()')

        def on_message_complete(self):
            print('on_message_complete()')

Output:

```
data_received()
on_headers_complete()
on_message_complete()
upgrade exception
```

On first sight this looks awkward to resolve, so it could just be considered a constraint of the implementation, and deal with it in python-land instead (which would be acceptable from my POV)

issue_title: Confusion in Snake/Camel Case Plugin Argument
issue_body:
Graphene's Default Behavior is something like this:

Query:
```
{
    jobs(id: "123918239", jobId: 2) {
        ...
        ...
    }
}
```
```
for i in args:
    print i

>> id
>> job_id
```

With the plugin argument of:

```
schema = graphene.Schema(
    query=QueryType,
    auto_camelcase=False
)
```
```
for i in args:
    print i

>> id
>> jobId
```

Now isn't the plugin argument naming erroneous?
Printing `snake_case` by default is fine. But not printing `snake_case` when `auto_camelcase=False` is confusing. It should be `auto_snakecase=False` that stops the automatic `snake_casing`.

issue_title: tflearn.embedding returns a list of tensors instead of 3d tensor
issue_body:
I try to use tflearn.flatten() after tflearn.embedding:

`
net1 = tflearn.input_data([None, 100])
net1 = tflearn.embedding(net1, input_dim=10000, output_dim=128)
tflearn.flatten(net1)
`

However, I get an error "AssertionError: Incoming Tensor shape must be at least 2-D". This doesnt look too weird, because the embedding function seems to return a list of tensors instead of a 3d tensor.

It is a modification of the IMDB sentiment example by @aymericdamien, which does work. (the lstm function doesnt seem to care about this, although it required the same input).

issue_title: opts.yticklabels are ignored
issue_body:
**Bug Description**
Please enter a clear and concise description of what the bug is.
The docs say I can use `opt.xticklabels` and `opt.yticklabels` in some functions. I'd like to use them in heatmap.

**Reproduction Steps**
```
viz = visdom.Visdom()
X = np.random.rand(2, 4)
viz.heatmap(X, win='my_win', opts=dict(
    xtickslabels=['x0', 'x1', 'x2', 'x3'],
    yticklabels=['women', 'men'],
))
```

**Expected behavior**
Heatmap with specified x- and y-labels as strings.

**Got**
Heatmap with default integer-based x- and y-labels. Labels are ignored.

This may be beyond the scope of what cmd2 is intended to provide.  But if there was an easy way to add this functionality, it would be useful.

issue_title: Bound checks in numpy array accessors
issue_body:
The current behavior of data access methods in `py::array` and `py::array_t` is to check the dimension bounds every time the accessor is called, via the `check_dimensions` function, event when using it in a loop.

I agree that when exposing a feature to Python, it must be written in such a way that regardless of the values passed to it, it cannot crash the interpreter, but these accessors are not directly exposed to the Python users. Instead, I think that one of the main reasons people would right c++ extensions with pybind is because they want *speed*, and they might be disappointed with this behavior. https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/500 already improved the situation, but as we discussed there, I would prefer to completely remove the bound checking.

issue_title: Iterators efficiency and instance tracking

issue_body:

`certbot certificates -d example.com` should only list certs for that domain (though question: if the user specifies two domains, should it be intersection or union?)

`certbot certificates --cert-name NAME` should only provide information about that specific lineage.

issue_title: Standalone mode/manual mode does not bind to ipv6
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, the letsencrypt client does not bind to ipv6 in standalone mode nor do the commands given in manual mode. While this may not seem too significant, but for people with ipv6 only machines (like me) this is quite a big problem. My suggestion would be to somehow bind to both ipv4 and ipv6 at the same time.

Either way, what you're doing is a great service to the global internet community, keep on rocking!

issue_title: Make key and secret optional for tool provider
issue_body:
Tool Providers need to implement a `RequestValidator` in order to interface with the signing logic in OAuthlib. Prefetching the secret, based on the key provided, is both unnecessary, and potentially dangerous. The `RequestValidator`, while annoying, provides an excellent framework for avoiding timing attacks, and requiring the secret to be retrieved outside of that validator is asking for implementors to do it wrong and open themselves up to these timing attacks.

At this juncture I don't wish to make any backward incompatible changes, so my thought is to make them optional for both the `ToolProvider` and `ToolBase` classes, but still required for `ToolConsumer`, and have the key and secret set during validation in the tool provider.

To avoid needing to call the validator's `get_client_secret` method twice, because it may involve database operations that we'd rather not do twice, I'm thinking to make a proxy validator that will simply proxy all of it's attribute access to the real validator, but cache the client secret so that it can be retrieved afterward.

issue_title: Task option map_vars
issue_body:
There are four potential methods to adapt tasks to be executed remotely.

1. explicit `to_host` function, tried but gave up, too troublesome to use.
2. SoS handles `_input` etc and `map_vars` lists the rest
3. SoS tries to convert all variables.
4. SoS lists variables that should not be translated.

I. I gave up 1 because it is difficult to use and problems with remote signature.
2. Current implementation because we know `_input` are PATH, and users controls the rest
3. This is very tempting because we have a fixed list of variables to pass to task and it is not terribly difficult to translate them all. But it is likely that some of the variables should be kept in tact.
4. A remedy for the fear if 3 is adopted.

Note that there is a technique problem with 3. Currently we convert all path to absolute path and apply path maps, so **any string would be converted even if it is not a path** (`sample_name` in our example will be converted). So the difference between 2 and 4 is if we should specify additional variables to be mapped, or not mapped.

issue_title: In internal global state API, always use task_table_test_and_update.
issue_body:
Right now we have implemented two closely related commands in the internal global state API.

```cpp
/**
 * Update a task's scheduling information in the task table. This assumes that
 * the task spec already exists in the task table entry.
 *
 * @param db_handle Database handle.
 * @param task The task entry to add to the table. The task spec in the entry is
 *        ignored.
 * @param retry Information about retrying the request to the database.
 * @param done_callback Function to be called when database returns result.
 * @param user_context Data that will be passed to done_callback and
 *        fail_callback.
 * @return Void.
 */
void task_table_update(DBHandle *db_handle,
                       OWNER Task *task,
                       RetryInfo *retry,
                       task_table_done_callback done_callback,
                       void *user_context);

/**
 * Update a task's scheduling information in the task table, if the current
 * value matches the given test value. If the update succeeds, it also updates
 * the task entry's local scheduler ID with the ID of the client who called
 * this function. This assumes that the task spec already exists in the task
 * table entry.
 *
 * @param db_handle Database handle.
 * @param task_id The task ID of the task entry to update.
 * @param test_state_bitmask The bitmask to apply to the task entry's current
 *        scheduling state.  The update happens if and only if the current
 *        scheduling state AND-ed with the bitmask is greater than 0.
 * @param update_state The value to update the task entry's scheduling state
 *        with, if the current state matches test_state_bitmask.
 * @param retry Information about retrying the request to the database.
 * @param done_callback Function to be called when database returns result.
 * @param user_context Data that will be passed to done_callback and
 *        fail_callback.
 * @return Void.
 */
void task_table_test_and_update(
    DBHandle *db_handle,
    TaskID task_id,
    int test_state_bitmask,
    int update_state,
    RetryInfo *retry,
    task_table_test_and_update_callback done_callback,
    void *user_context);
```

The difference is that `task_table_test_and_update` atomically performs the update conditional upon the current state being one of the states listed in `test_state_bitmask`. The motivation for introducing `task_table_test_and_update` was to prevent multiple local schedulers from simultaneously trying to reconstruct a task by allowing only one of them to transition the task status from `TASK_STATUS_LOST` to `TASK_STATUS_RECONSTRUCTING`.

However, it may make sense to use `task_table_test_and_update` everywhere that we are currently using `task_table_update`. This would allow us to catch bugs when the task state transitions in unexpected ways. It would also help with the implementation of task cancelation (see #854) because (e.g., the global scheduler or a local scheduler would try to change the task state and would fail, it would realize that the task was canceled and could act differently).

cc @stephanie-wang

issue_title: Appreciation
issue_body:
Since I just filed a bunch of nitpicky issues, I wanted to also stop and mention two things I really liked:

* The parallel initialization of fixtures in `_resolve_coroutine_fixtures_in` is awesome

* You fixed MultiError tracebacks! I know it's still not as pretty as the regular pytest tracebacks, but I have wasted so much time trying to guess the meaning of incomplete MultiError tracebacks from pytest, and I also spent like an hour once trying to figure out how to hook traceback printing and totally failed. So: thank you!

Thanks.

issue_title: Dockerhub run documentation gone

A while ago, there was a guide to run localstack with one command 
docker run -it -p 4567-4578:4567-4578 -p 8080:8080 atlassianlabs/localstack

issue_title: 拿到元素后，get其中的text，中文字符"周生生"拿到的是\u5468\u751f\u751f\u4eac\u4e1c\u65d7\u8230\u5e97
Earley parser produces non-deterministic results even with explicit terminal weights
issue_body:
Perhaps related to #191 

When using the earley parser with weighted terminals, the result is still non-deterministic in certain cases.

Example:
```
from lark import Lark

grammar = """
root: A | B

A.1: ("0".."9")+
B.2: ("0".."9")+

%import common.WS
%ignore WS
"""

parser = Lark(grammar, start="root", parser="earley")
print(parser.parse("10"))
```

It is my understanding that using weights was a workaround for this non-determinism (as indicated in #167), but this does not seem to be the case.

I am using the latest lark version available on pip (0.6.2) on Fedora Linux 27 x86_64.
